governing_equations: Time of flight T = 2 v \sin(\theta) / g
Complementary angles \theta and (90^\circ - \theta) yield the same range for a given v

socratic_plan.question: Before we launch anything — draw on the canvas where you think the ball will go. Start from the cannon and trace the path you expect it to follow.
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student externalises their mental model of projectile motion before observing it, revealing misconceptions about arc shape or landing point

socratic_plan.question: What do you think will happen to the range if you increase the launch angle?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student predicts the angle-range relationship before observing it
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student makes a prediction or states uncertainty

socratic_plan.question: At what angle do you think the ball will travel the farthest? Make a guess.
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student forms a hypothesis about the optimal launch angle
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student gives a numeric guess for the optimal angle

socratic_plan.question: Try 30°, then try 60°. What do you notice about the ranges?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student discovers the complementary-angle symmetry empirically
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student notices the ranges are equal or nearly equal

socratic_plan.question: Why would 30° and 60° give the same range? What is the same and what is different between those two flights?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student reasons about the horizontal vs vertical velocity trade-off
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student connects the symmetry to the velocity component relationship

annotate.text: Max height driven by vertical speed

verification.summary: Complementary angles 30° and 60° share range; 45° maximizes range at fixed v; time of flight rises with launch angle below 90°.

probes.description: 30° launch at 25 m/s — complementary pair leg
60° launch at 25 m/s — complementary pair leg
45° launch at 25 m/s — near-maximum range check

invariants.description: 30° and 60° landings should report nearly the same range (complementary-angle symmetry).
At 45° and 25 m/s, horizontal range should match v²/g.
